revision.text:
[[en:Ame-no-Minakanushi]][[es:Ame-no-minaka-nushi-no-kami]][[fa:آمه-نو-میناکانوشی]][[fr:Ame-no-Minakanushi]][[id:Ame-no-Minakanushi]][[it:Ame-no-Minakanushi]][[ja:天之御中主神]][[mni:ꯑꯃꯦ-ꯅꯣ-ꯃꯤꯅꯀꯅꯨꯁꯤ]][[pl:Amenominakanushi]][[ru:Амэноминакануси]][[simple:Ame-no-Minakanushi]][[uz:Ame-no Minakanusi]][[zh:天之御中主神]] {{Short description|Japanese deity|qq=}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Ame-no-Minakanushi | image = Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg | caption = Creation of the world according to the ''[[Kojiki]]'', showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 天之御中主神, 天御中主神 | other_names = Ame-no-Minakanushi-no-Mikoto (天御中主尊) | cult_center = [[Chiba Shrine]], [[Kurume Suitengū]], and others | symbols = | consort = None | parents = None; self-generated | siblings = None | children = None | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'', ''[[:en:Sendai Kuji Hongi|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' }} '''Ame-no-Minakanushi''' (天之御中主, lit. "Lord of the August Center of Heaven"{{cite book |editor1-last=Breen |editor1-first=John |editor2-last=Teeuwen |editor2-first=Mark |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |date=2013 |publisher=Routledge |page=48 |isbn=9781136826979 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DR2PAQAAQBAJ&pg=PA48|qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Ooms |first1=Herman |title=Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800 |date=2009 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |page=168 |isbn=9780824832353 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&pg=PA168|qq=}}) is a deity (''[[kami]]'') in [[Japanese mythology]], portrayed in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' as the first or one of [[Kotoamatsukami|the first deities]] who manifested when heaven and earth came into existence. ==Name== The ''kami'' is given the name 'Ame-no-Minakanushi' (天之御中主神; {{ill|Old Japanese|en|Old Japanese|qid=Q5736700|lt=Old Japanese|1=Old Japanese}}: ''Ame[[Old Japanese#Syllables|2]]-no2-Mi1nakanusi''{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |year=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |page=457|qq=}}) in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (ca. 712 CE). The same deity is referred to as 'Ame-no-Minakanushi-no-Mikoto' (天御中主尊) in a variant account cited in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (720 CE). ==Mythology== The ''Kojiki'' portrays Ame-no-Minakanushi as the first god to appear in the heavenly realm of [[Takamagahara]] after the emergence of heaven and earth from the primeval {{ill|Chaos (cosmogony)|en|Chaos (cosmogony)|qid=Q17508277|lt=chaos|1=Chaos (cosmogony)}}: {{quotation|At the time of the beginning of heaven and earth, there came into existence in Takamanohara a deity named Ame-no-Minakanushi; next, [[Takamimusubi|Takamimusubi-no-Kami]]; next, [[Kamimusubi|Kamimusubi-no-Kami]]. These three deities all came into existence as single deities (''{{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|qid=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}}''), and their forms were not visible (or 'they hid their bodies').Translation from {{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |year=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |page=49|qq=}} Names (transcribed in {{ill|Old Japanese|en|Old Japanese|qid=Q5736700|lt=Old Japanese|1=Old Japanese}} in the original) have been changed into their modern equivalents.|qq=}} Unlike later generations of ''kami'', the first seven gods were "single" or "solitary" in that they came into being one by one, without any counterparts, and are described as hiding their presence upon coming into existence. Ame-no-Minakanushi is reckoned as the first of the "three deities of creation" (造化三神, ''zōka sanshin'') and one of the five "distinguished heavenly gods" (別天津神, ''[[kotoamatsukami]]'').{{cite web |last1=Mori |first1=Mizue |title=Amenominakanushi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=38 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-11-07|qq=}} In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'''s main narrative and many of the variant accounts cited in it, the first ''kami'' is identified instead as [[Kuni-no-Tokotachi]]; Ame-no-Minakanushi only appears in passing in one of these variants: {{quotation|In one writing it is said:—"When Heaven and Earth began, there were Deities produced together, whose names were, first, Kuni-no-toko-tachi no Mikoto, and next [[:ja:国狭槌尊|Kuni no sa-tsuchi no Mikoto]]." It is further stated:—"The names of the Gods which were produced in the Plain of High Heaven were Ama no mi-naka-nushi no Mikoto, next Taka-mi-musubi no Mikoto, next Kami-mi-musubi no Mikoto."|author=translation by [[William George Aston]]{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=5|qq=}}|qq=}} In the ''[[:en:Sendai Kuji Hongi|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'', the first deity to appear is named 'Ame-Yuzuruhi-Ame-no-Sagiri-Kuni-Yuzurutsuki-Kuni-no-Sagiri-no-Mikoto' (天譲日天狭霧国禅月国狭霧尊). Here, Ame-no-Minakanushi - given the alias 'Ame-no-Tokotachi-no-Mikoto' (天常立尊; the name of a distinct ''kami'' in the ''Kojiki'') - along with the deity {{ill|Umashiashikabihikoji|en|Umashiashikabihikoji|qid=Q10946168|lt=Umashi-Ashikabi-Hikoji|1=Umashi-Ashikabi-Hikoji}} (宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神) is instead counted as the first generation that emerged after this god.{{cite web |last1=Kadoya |first1=Atsushi |title=Ameyuzuruhiamenosagirikuniyuzuruhikuninosagiri |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=47 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-11-01|qq=}}{{cite book |editor=Keizai Zasshi-sha |title=国史大系 第7巻 (Kokushi Taikei, vol. 7) |date=1898 |publisher=Keizai Zasshi-sha |pages=173–178 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/991097/100 |chapter=先代旧事本紀 巻第一 神代本紀 (Sendai Kuji Hongi, vol. 1: Jindai Hongi)|qq=}}{{cite web |title=巻第一 神代本紀・神代系紀・陰陽本紀 |url=http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~xx8f-ishr/sendaikuji_1.htm |website=『先代旧事本紀』の現代語訳（ＨＩＳＡＳＨＩ） |access-date=2020-11-01|qq=}} No further mention is made of Ame-no-Minakanushi in these texts. {| class="wikitable collapsible collapsed" style="margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto; border: none; text-align: center;" ! !! scope="col" style="width: 160px;" |''Kojiki''!! scope="col" style="width: 160px;" |''Nihon Shoki''
(main text) !! scope="col" style="width: 150px;" |''Nihon Shoki''
(variant 2)!! scope="col" style="width: 160px;" |''Nihon Shoki''
(variant 4)!! scope="col" style="width: 160px;" |''Nihon Shoki''
(variant 6) !! scope="col" style="width: 160px;" |''Sendai Kuji Hongi'' |- ! scope="row" | The first generations of ''kami'' | (''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'')
1. Ame-no-Minakanushi
2. [[Takamimusubi]]
3. [[Kamimusubi]]
4. Umashi-Ashikabi-Hikoji
5. Ame-no-Tokotachi

([[Kamiyonanayo|Seven divine generations]])
1. Kuni-no-Tokotachi
2. Toyokumono
3. Uijini, Suijini
4. Tsunugui, Ikugui
5. Ōtonoji, Ōtonobe
6. Omodaru, Ayakashikone
7. [[Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} || (Seven divine generations)
1. Kuni-no-Tokotachi
2. [[Kuni-no-Satsuchi]]
3. Toyokumunu
4. Uijini, Suijini
5. Ōtonoji, Ōtomabe
6. Omodaru, Kashikone
7. Izanagi, Izanami || 1. Umashi-Ashikabi-Hikoji
2. Kuni-no-Tokotachi
3. Kuni-no-Satsuchi || 1. Kuni-no-Tokotachi
2.Kuni no Satsuchi

(Emerged in Takamagahara)
1. Ame-no-Minakanushi
2. Takamimusubi
3. Kamimusubi|| 1. Ame-no-Tokotachi
2. Umashi-Ashikabi-Hikoji || Ame-Yuzuruhi-Ame-no-Sagiri-Kuni-Yuzurutsuki-Kuni-no-Sagiri

(Seven divine generations)
1. Ame-no-Minakanushi, Umashi-Ashikabi-Hikoji
2. Kuni-no-Tokotachi, Toyokuninushi (Toyokumunu) + Ame-no-Yakudari
3. Tsugunui, Ikugui + Ame-no-Mikudari
4. Uijini, Suijini + Ameai
5. Ōtonoji, Ōtomabe + Ame-no-Yaohi
6. Aokashikine (Omotaru), Ayakashikine (Kashikone) + Ame-no-Yasorodama
7. [[Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} + Takamimusubi, Kamimusubi, Ikumusubi, Tsuhayamusubi, Furutama, Yorotama |} ===Descendants=== An imperially commissioned genealogical record known as the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|qid=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' (815 CE) identifies two clans as the progeny of deities descended from Ame-no-Minakanushi:{{cite web |title=『新撰姓氏録』氏族一覧２（第二帙／神別） |url=http://kitagawa.la.coocan.jp/data/shoji02.html |website=北川研究室 |access-date=2020-11-04 |language=ja|qq=}} *The Hattori no [[Kabane|Muraji]] (服部連) clan, descended from Ame-no-Mihoko (天御桙命), the 11th generation descendant of Ame-no-Minakanushi *The Miteshiro no Obito (御手代首) clan, descended from Ame-no-Minakanushi's 10th generation descendant Ame-no-Morokami (天諸神命) == Analysis == {{see also|Tian|Shangdi|Sky deity|Myōken|qq=}} [[File:妙見菩薩（『仏像図彙』より）- Myōken Bosatsu (from the Butsuzōzui).jpg|thumb|left|160px|{{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|qid=Q10960623|lt=Myōken|1=Myōken}}, the Buddhist deification of the {{ill|Polaris|en|Polaris|qid=Q12980|lt=North Star|1=Polaris}} and/or the {{ill|Big Dipper|en|Big Dipper|qid=Q10460|lt=Big Dipper|1=Big Dipper}}]] There is no extant undisputed record of Ame-no-Minakanushi being worshiped at any known ancient shrines (the ''[[Engishiki]]'', compiled in the early 10th century, never mentions any shrines to this deity); this, combined with the lack of information concerning the god outside of documents associated with the imperial court such as ''Kojiki'' and the ''Shoki'' (as seen above, even in these texts, barely any mention is made of this god), has led some scholars to consider Ame-no-Minakanushi to be an abstract deity (i.e. a god that only exists on paper, with no actual worshipers or {{ill|Cult (religious practice)|en|Cult (religious practice)|qid=Q756820|lt=cult|1=Cult (religious practice)}} dedicated to him) created under the influence of {{ill|Chinese philosophy|en|Chinese philosophy|qid=Q184663|lt=Chinese thought|1=Chinese philosophy}}.{{cite web |title=天之御中主神 (Ame-no-Minakanushi) |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A9%E4%B9%8B%E5%BE%A1%E4%B8%AD%E4%B8%BB%E7%A5%9E-1129356 |website=コトバンク (Kotobank) |publisher=The Asahi Shimbun Company, VOYAGE MARKETING |access-date=2020-11-01|qq=}} Other scholars, however, argue that the paucity of evidence for the worship of Ame-no-Minakanushi in antiquity does not necessarily mean that the god is purely a literary invention.{{cite book |last1=Katō |first1=Genchi |title=A Study of Shinto: The Religion of the Japanese Nation |date=2010 |publisher=Routledge |pages=28–30 |isbn=9781136903700 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MeO3BgAAQBAJ&pg=PA29|qq=}}{{cite journal |last1=Kishine |first1=Toshiyuki |title=日本神話におけるアメノミナカヌシ（I） (Amenominakanushi in Japanese Mythology (1)) |journal=福岡大学人文論叢 (Fukuoka University Review of Literature & Humanities) |date=2009 |volume=41 |issue=1 |pages=515–551 |url=https://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110007119640|qq=}}{{cite journal |last1=Kishine |first1=Toshiyuki |title=日本神話におけるアメノミナカヌシ（II） (Amenominakanushi in Japanese Mythology (2))|journal=福岡大学人文論叢 (Fukuoka University Review of Literature & Humanities) |date=2009 |volume=41 |issue=2 |pages=905–944 |url=https://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110007318018|qq=}} [[:ja:小西甚一|Konishi Jin'ichi]] (1984) saw the creation narratives of the ''Kojiki'' and the ''Shoki'' as a combination of three different traditions: one which traces the origin of the gods to Ame-no-Minakanushi, another that began with Umashi-Ashikabi-Hikoji, and a third one starting with Kuni-no-Tokotachi. He saw similarities between Ame-no-Minakanushi and the {{ill|Sky deity|en|Sky deity|qid=Q6639573|lt=sky deities|1=Sky deity}} {{ill|Tangaloa|en|Tangaloa|qid=Q7682799|lt=Tangaloa|1=Tangaloa}} ([[:en:Polynesian narrative|Polynesian mythology]]) and {{ill|Tengri|en|Tengri|qid=Q2481517|lt=Tengri|1=Tengri}} ({{ill|Turkic mythology|en|Turkic mythology|qid=Q588796|lt=Turkic|1=Turkic mythology}} and [[Mongol mythology]]), suggesting that these myths may ultimately share a common origin.{{cite book |last1=Konishi |first1=Jin'ichi |title=A History of Japanese Literature, Volume 1: The Archaic and Ancient Ages |date=2017 |publisher=Princeton University Press |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=k4orDgAAQBAJ&pg=PA170 |pages=170–173|isbn=9781400886333 |qq=}} {{ill|Hayao Kawai|en|Hayao Kawai|qid=Q2025444|lt=Kawai Hayao|1=Hayao Kawai}} compared Ame-no-Minakanushi with the moon deity [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] and [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Hosuseri]] (one of [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Konohanasakuyahime]]'s three children), in that all three are portrayed as belonging to a triad of important deities and yet are not recorded as doing anything of significance. He considered these three 'inactive' deities to serve a mythic function as the 'hollow center' acting as a buffer zone between two opposite or conflicting forces (Kamimusubi and Takamimusubi, [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], {{ill|Hoderi|en|Hoderi|qid=Q2716491|lt=Hoderi|1=Hoderi}} and [[Hoori]]).{{cite journal |last1=Kawai |first1=Hayao |last2=Hori |first2=Tadashi |title=The Hollow Center in the Mythology of Kojiki. |journal=Review of Japanese Culture and Society |date=1986 |volume=1 |issue=1 |pages=72–77 |jstor=42800067 |url=http://www.jstor.org/stable/42800067|qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Kawai |first1=Hayao |title=中空構造日本の深層 (Chūkū kōzō Nihon no shinsō) |date=1999 |publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|qq=}} ===During the medieval and early modern periods=== Until the medieval era, the ''Nihon Shoki'', owing to its status as one of the [[Rikkokushi|six national histories]], was more widely read and commented upon than the ''Kojiki'', which was regarded as an ancillary work. In a similar vein, the ''Sendai Kuji Hongi'', due to its preface claiming it to be compiled by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} and [[Soga no Umako]], was seen as being earlier and more reliable. (Modern consensus holds the ''Kuji Hongi'' to actually have been compiled during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, although certain portions of it may indeed preserve genuine early traditions.){{cite book |last1=Saitō |first1=Hideki |title=古事記 不思議な1300th year 史 [Kojiki: Fushigi na 1300 nen shi] |date=2012 |publisher=Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha |language=ja|pages=36–77|qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |year=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=30–32|qq=}} References to Ame-no-Minakanushi were thus solely in terms of his role as one of the primeval ''kami''. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the school of thought developed by the [[:ja:渡会氏|Watarai]] priestly house of the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Outer Shrine of Ise]] (''Gekū'') known as [[:ja:伊勢神道|Ise Shinto]] (also known as Watarai Shinto), identified the shrine's deity, {{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|qid=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke|1=Toyouke-hime}} (Toyoukehime), with Ame-no-Minakanushi and Kuni-no-Tokotachi. By doing so, the Outer Shrine asserted superiority over the Inner Shrine (''Naikū'') and its goddess, [[Amaterasu]].{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |pages=169–171 |isbn=9780190621711 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ&pg=PA169|qq=}} It was upon the flourishing of nativist studies (''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|qid=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}}'') and the rediscovery and reappraisal of the ''Kojiki'' in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} that Ame-no-Minakanushi's significance was reevaluated, with different authors expressing their own opinions on the god's role and importance. [[File:Motoori Norinaga self portrait.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} (self-portrait)]] {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}}, in his {{ill|Kojiki-den|en|Kojiki-den|qid=Q11410835|lt=commentary on the ''Kojiki''|1=Kojiki-den}}, criticized the Watarai priesthood for laying emphasis on the importance of Ame-no-Minakanushi, arguing that the deities' order of appearance in time has no bearing on their Rank or status. In his view, while Ame-no-Minakanushi is indeed the first among the gods to manifest, he is neither the ruler of heaven nor the "first ancestor" of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=imperial line|1=Imperial House of Japan}} (that being [[Amaterasu]]), as some in his time believed. Motoori instead characterized Takamimusubi and Kamimusubi as the "first ancestors of heaven and earth, of the ''kami'', and of all existence."{{cite web |last1=Sasaki |first1=Kiyoshi |title=Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/cpjr/kami/sasaki.html |website=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-11-07|qq=}} [[:ja:鶴峯戊申|Tsurumine Shigenobu]] (1788-1859), who attempted to make a rational interpretation of the creation myths of the ''Kojiki'' and ''Shoki'' based on a synthesis with his understanding of European science and astronomy, associated Ame-no-Minakanushi with {{ill|Gravity|en|Gravity|qid=Q11412|lt=Gravity|1=Gravity}}: {{quotation|These three ''kami'' are first, Amanominakanushi, secondly Takamimusubi, and thirdly, Kamimusubi. These three great ''kami'' are described in the body of the ''Kojiki'' with the words, "These three ''kami'' were produced alone, and hid themselves." The meaning of "hid themselves" is that they could not be perceived by human eyes. The fact that there are particles [''bunshi''] in all things is probably a result of the action of the [''kami''] of productive power (Mimusubi), while the fact there is gravity is a result of the action of [the ''kami''] Minakanushi. This gravity cannot be seen with eyes or taken up in the hand, and like the attraction between magnets and iron, is something that mutually pulls and attracts things together. It is because of the existence of this gravity that particles coalesce and thus the sun, moon, planets and earth spontaneously take their proper form. As a result, when one speaks of the "land illumined with heavenly crimson shining," it was due to the actions of these three ''kami'' that [that land] came into being. Thus, while these three ''kami'' are said to have become in the Plain of High Heaven, it does not mean literally there was originally a place called the Plain of High Heaven, and that the ''kami'' then came into being within that place, but rather that the Plain of High Heaven itself emerged into existence by virtue of the fact that these three ''kami'' came into being. Now, when one speaks of Amenominakanushi no kami, the ''amenominaka'' means around the core of the sun, while ''nushi'' means the lord of that place, with result that the name means the one who makes its domain in heaven and earth.|qq=}} By linking gravity to Ame-no-Minakanushi, Tsurumine identifies the deity as the "lord" who oversees the process whereby the activity of the two gods of "coalescing" (''musubi'') results in the creation of all things out of the basic elements represented by "particles." Using language apparently borrowed from {{ill|Christianity|en|Christianity|qid=Q5043|lt=Christian|1=Christianity}} conceptions of {{ill|God in Christianity|en|God in Christianity|qid=Q825|lt=God|1=God in Christianity}}, Tsurumine then went on to describe the three deities of creation as "the ancient ancestral ''kami'' of heaven and great ''kami'' sovereign over first origins ... who have made all things, from sun and moon, the planets, and earth to every other thing." Motoori's admirer and self-proclaimed disciple [[Hirata Atsutane]], in contrast to Motoori, described Ame-no-Minakanushi as a supreme deity with no beginning and no end who holds sovereignty over all existence, residing in the {{ill|Polaris|en|Polaris|qid=Q12980|lt=pole star|1=Polaris}} at the very center of heaven. {{quotation|All things in heaven and earth have an original, great ancestral ''kami''. Its name is Amenominakanushi no kami. It has no beginning and no end. It abides in the heavens above. It is furnished with the quality of producing all things within heaven and earth, but it acts not and is quiescent (it dwells in the so-called Plain of High Heaven from the original beginning), and it is sovereign over all existence. Next are Takamimusubi no kami and Kamimusubi no kami. They are apportioned with the qualities of Amenominakanushi no kami, producing all things in heaven and earth, and exerting sovereignty over all things in heaven and earth. They are equipped with the immeasurably marvelous quality called the spirit of generation (''musubi''). They are the ultimate natural ancestral kami of human beings. These two ancestral deities then forged heaven and earth, giving birth to [[Izanagi|Izanagi no mikoto]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami no mikoto|1=Izanami}}, and making them firm the land and give birth to the people. These are our great natural parent deities.|qq=}} A number of Hirata's disciples, meanwhile, came to formulate different understandings of Ame-no-Minakanushi from their mentor. One such disciple, [[:ja:六人部是香|Mutobe Yoshika]] (1798-1864), for instance argued that all the stars in the sky have their own {{ill|Planetary system|en|Planetary system|qid=Q206717|lt=Planetary system|1=Planetary system}} similar to the {{ill|Sun|en|Sun|qid=Q525|lt=Sun|1=Sun}}; the ''Kojiki'''s description of the generation of heaven and Earth thus does not refer to the entire cosmos as Hirata interpreted it, but the {{ill|Solar System|en|Solar System|qid=Q544|lt=Solar System|1=Solar System}} (which Mutobe equates with the mythical Takamagahara) alone. According to Mutobe, all the stars with their respective planetary system were formed by the three deities of creation, who then came down to dwell in our Solar System. Unlike Hirata, Mutobe relegated Ame-no-Minakanushi and the other two deities of creation to a minor role and instead accorded high status to the earthly deity [[Ōkuninushi]], who he argued was given jurisdiction by the gods Takamimusubi and [[Amaterasu]] not only over the lives and fates of human beings but also over grains and other foods. He thus took the evaluation Hirata had given to the three ''kami'' of creation and reapplied it to Ōkuninushi, essentially elevating him to a kind of supreme deity. ==Worship== {{One source section | date = August 2021 }} The Buddhist deity {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|qid=Q10960623|lt=Myōken|1=Myōken}}, who was worshiped in Japan since at least the 7th century, became conflated with Ame-no-Minakanushi during the early modern period due to Myōken's association with the {{ill|Big Dipper|en|Big Dipper|qid=Q10460|lt=Big Dipper|1=Big Dipper}} and the {{ill|Polaris|en|Polaris|qid=Q12980|lt=northern pole star|1=Polaris}}. When the Meiji government mandated the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]], many shrines dedicated to Myōken became shrines to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Ame-no-Minakanushi was also one of the patron deities of the {{Nihongo|[[Taikyo Institute]]|大教院|Taikyoin|qq=}}, a short-lived government organization that promoted a state-sponsored fusion of Buddhism and Shinto after the earlier separation policy was deemed as being too divisive. ==See also== *[[Kuni-no-Tokotachi]] *{{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|qid=Q10960623|lt=Myōken|1=Myōken}} *{{ill|Suitengū|en|Suitengū|qid=Q125319456|lt=Suitengū|1=Suitengū}} *{{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|qid=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke-hime|1=Toyouke-hime}} * {{ill|Sky father|en|Sky father|qid=Q3512027|lt=Sky father|1=Sky father}} * {{ill|Taiji (philosophy)|en|Taiji (philosophy)|qid=Q53685|lt=Taiji|1=Taiji (philosophy)}} * {{ill|Yin and yang|en|Yin and yang|qid=Q62744|lt=Yin and yang|1=Yin and yang}} '''Counterparts of Ame-no-Minakanushi in other cultures''' * {{ill|Hiranyagarbha|en|Hiranyagarbha|qid=Q2075736|lt=Hiranyagarbha|1=Hiranyagarbha}}, the {{ill|Hinduism|en|Hinduism|qid=Q9089|lt=Hindu|1=Hinduism}} counterpart * {{ill|Jade Emperor|en|Jade Emperor|qid=Q860434|lt=Jade Emperor|1=Jade Emperor}}, the {{ill|Chinese theology|en|Chinese theology|qid=Q2928436|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese theology}} counterpart * [[:en:Adi Buddha|Adi Buddha]], the {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} counterpart * {{ill|Yuanshi Tianzun|en|Yuanshi Tianzun|qid=Q1195885|lt=Yuanshi Tianzun|1=Yuanshi Tianzun}}, the [[Taoism|Taoist]] counterpart * [[:en:Haneullim|Haneullim]], the {{ill|Korean mythology|en|Korean mythology|qid=Q702065|lt=Korean|1=Korean mythology}} counterpart * {{ill|Ông Trời|en|Ông Trời|qid=Q111181067|lt=Ông Trời|1=Ông Trời}}, the {{ill|Vietnamese mythology|en|Vietnamese mythology|qid=Q1195236|lt=Vietnamese|1=Vietnamese mythology}} counterpart ==References== {{Reflist|qq=}} ==Sources== * Joseph Mitsuo Kitagawa. ''On Understanding Japanese Religion''. Princeton University Press, 1987. {{ISBN|0691102295|qq=}} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp Encyclopedia of Shinto] *Amenominakanushi on the [https://www.historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenominakanushi Japanese History Database]. {{jmyth navbox long|qq=}} <{{Myōken Faith|qq=}} [[en:Ame-no-Minakanushi]] {{Commons category|Ame-no-Minakanushi}} [[es:Ame-no-minaka-nushi-no-kami]] [[fa:آمه-نو-میناکانوشی]] [[fr:Ame-no-Minakanushi]] [[id:Ame-no-Minakanushi]] [[ja:天之御中主神]] [[mni:ꯑꯃꯦ-ꯅꯣ-ꯃꯤꯅꯀꯅꯨꯁꯤ]] [[pl:Amenominakanushi]] [[ru:Амэноминакануси]] [[simple:Ame-no-Minakanushi]] [[uz:Ame-no Minakanusi]] [[zh:天之御中主神]] {{wikidata link|Q2842779}} [[Category:Creator deities]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:天空神]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:水天宮]] [[Category:独神]] [[Category:相馬神社]] [[Category:Pages with 50+ untranslated japanese characters]]
